name: Bow of Apollo
description: The Bow of Apollo is a legendary weapon, intrinsically linked to the Greek god of music, poetry, light, healing, and prophecy. Forged by Hephaestus, the divine smith, from materials said to be imbued with the very essence of sunlight and solidified starlight, it is far more than a mere hunting tool. Its arrows are unerringly accurate, capable of delivering both swift death and sudden plagues, as well as healing and purification, reflecting Apollo's dual nature. The bow's string, woven from golden threads spun by the Moirai (Fates), sings with a celestial harmony when drawn, a sound that can inspire courage in allies and instill terror in enemies. It is described as gleaming with an inner light, radiating warmth and power. Only those deemed worthy by Apollo himself can wield the bow effectively; for others, it proves unwieldy and unresponsive. In some accounts, the arrows are tipped with the venom of the Python, a monstrous serpent slain by Apollo, adding to their lethal potency. The bow symbolizes divine power, precision, and the duality of creation and destruction. Its appearance often heralds significant events, both joyous and catastrophic, and its presence signifies Apollo's direct intervention in mortal affairs. The bow is not merely an object; it's an extension of Apollo's will, a conduit for his divine energy, and a testament to his multifaceted dominion. Its existence underscores the Greek belief in the gods' active involvement in the human world and the profound consequences of their actions.
subtitle: Divine Weapon of Light and Plague
currentLocation: Unknown, possibly in Apollo's possession or lost to time
associatedHeroes: None specifically mentioned, though some myths imply heroes received aid via the bow

keyMyths.title: Apollo's Slaying of Python
keyMyths.description: Apollo used his bow and arrows to slay the monstrous serpent Python, who guarded the Oracle of Delphi. This act established Apollo's control over Delphi and his prophetic abilities.

keyMyths.title: The Plague on the Achaean Army
keyMyths.description: In the Iliad, Apollo unleashes a plague upon the Achaean army with his arrows as punishment for Agamemnon's disrespect towards his priest, Chryses. This demonstrates the bow's destructive capabilities.

sources.author: Homer
sources.description: Depicts Apollo using his bow to inflict plague.
sources.text: Depicts Apollo using his bow to inflict plague.

sources.author: Homeric Hymns
sources.description: Describes Apollo's birth and his slaying of Python with his bow.
